name: Tengu Full
description: The Tengu, in Japanese folklore, are a type of legendary creature or supernatural being. They are often depicted with both human and avian characteristics. The most well-known depiction of Tengu is that of the Karasu Tengu (crow tengu), which have bird-like features such as wings and beaks. However, the more powerful and respected Tengu are the Yamabushi Tengu, who are often depicted with long noses and a more human-like appearance. Tengu are often associated with the mountains and forests, and they are sometimes seen as protectors of these wild places. They are known for their mastery of martial arts and magical abilities, and they are often depicted wielding a shakujo (a Buddhist staff) or a sword. Tengu can be mischievous, tricking humans or leading them astray, but they can also be benevolent, offering guidance or protection to those they deem worthy. The Tengu's dual nature reflects the complex relationship between humanity and the natural world in Japanese mythology. They are powerful beings to be respected, feared, and sometimes even sought out for assistance.
subtitle: Bird-like mountain spirits of Japan
appearance: Tengu possess a blend of human and avian features. Karasu Tengu have crow-like heads with black feathers, sharp beaks, and wings. Yamabushi Tengu are more human-like, with long noses, red faces, and often wear the garb of mountain ascetics. They are typically depicted as tall and powerful, with piercing eyes and a commanding presence. Some depictions show them with feathered wings, while others omit them. Their clothing often reflects their association with the mountains and forests.
habitat: Tengu are primarily found in mountainous regions and dense forests throughout Japan. They are believed to inhabit remote and secluded areas, far from human settlements. Certain mountains are considered to be particularly sacred to the Tengu, such as Mount Kurama near Kyoto.
behavior: Tengu are known for their unpredictable and often mischievous behavior. They can be helpful guides to lost travelers, but they are also known to trick and deceive those who disrespect the mountains. They are fiercely independent and do not easily tolerate interference in their domain. Tengu are highly skilled martial artists and are said to possess supernatural powers, which they use to protect their territory and enforce their will.

keyMyths.title: Minamoto no Yoshitsune and the Tengu
keyMyths.description: The legendary warrior Minamoto no Yoshitsune was said to have been trained in swordsmanship by the Tengu of Mount Kurama. This training allowed him to become one of the most skilled warriors in Japanese history, highlighting the Tengu's association with martial arts.

keyMyths.title: The Tengu's Fan
keyMyths.description: Tengu are often depicted carrying a fan made of feathers, which is said to have the power to create powerful gusts of wind. This fan symbolizes their control over the elements and their ability to influence the natural world.

sources.author: Carmen Blacker
sources.work: The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan
sources.description: Explores the shamanistic roots of Tengu beliefs.
sources.text: Explores the shamanistic roots of Tengu beliefs.

sources.author: Michael Dylan Foster
sources.work: The Book of Yokai: Mysterious Creatures of Japanese Folklore
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of various Yokai including the Tengu.
